---
title: "Demystifying Linear Regression"
sidebar: false
resources:
  - slides.pdf
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    page-layout: full
---

<div class="project-pdf-slides-root" hidden aria-hidden="true"></div>

:::: {.project-pdf-slides-intro}

One lecture’s worth of slides for McMaster geography undergraduates-**intuition before algebra**, with examples tied to how regression shows up in geographic research.

::::

## Project Overview

### Problem

The course enrolled geography students who were **not** required to take statistics first. They still needed to read papers and eventually run or interpret models, so regression could not stay a black box.

### Context

Undergraduate geography at McMaster: quantitative methods appear in readings and assignments, but comfort with formal stats varies widely across the cohort.

### Goals

- Give **usable intuition** for what linear regression does, what coefficients mean, and where models mislead.
- Use **visual**, small examples rather than long derivations.
- Connect content directly to **geographic** outcomes and common pitfalls (nonlinearity, outliers, confounding).

### Stakeholders

- **Students:** primary audience in the classroom.
- **Course instructor/program:** alignment with learning objectives and pacing for one lecture slot.

### Final deliverable

The **slide deck (PDF)** used in class-figures, talking points, and a progression from scatterplots through reading output and “what can go wrong.”

## Project Approach

### Scope & Assumptions

- **Scope (planned):** one lecture cycle-linear regression **basics**, light diagnostics, **geography-flavoured** examples.
- **Assumption:** I can demo software; I will **skip** long algebraic derivations.

### Data Sources

- **Planned:** small, **relatable** datasets; plots that show **fit**, **residuals**, and **model comparisons** at a glance.

### Analytical Design

- **Planned sequence:** least-squares **geometry** → what coefficients **mean** → pitfalls (nonlinearity, outliers, confounding).
- **Inference** stays light until intuition **lands**.

### Workflow / Process

**Planned arc:** motivation → scatterplots → model form → **how to read output** → geographic examples + **what can go wrong**.

### Constraints & Considerations

- **Class time** is fixed; math comfort will vary-I will pace for the **median** learner.
- Examples will **align** with how regression appears in their **readings and assignments**.

## Impact & Outcome

This lecture improved statistical confidence for students without prior quantitative training by making regression concepts approachable and visually intuitive. It supported more equitable learning outcomes and gave students a practical foundation for interpreting and applying models in geographic research contexts.

:::: {.pdf-slides-embed-bar}
[Open PDF in new tab](slides.pdf){.btn .btn-sm .btn-outline-primary target="_blank" rel="noopener"}
::::

:::: {#pdf-slides-frame-wrap .pdf-slides-embed-full}
<iframe src="slides.pdf" title="Transport geography - linear regression lecture slides (PDF)" class="pdf-slides-embed"></iframe>
::::
